Replace the .ToList()+Count()/Contains() let-clause patterns with server-translatable .Any() correlated subqueries (output-preserving; mirrors legacy intent: depdesg = statuses with no dept AND no desg rows; dep = statuses whose dept set contains the user's dept; etc.). Drop the `let` bindings.

  var dep = (from b in db.TaskStatus
             where db.TaskStatusDepts.Any(a => a.TaskStatusId == b.TaskStatusId && a.DeptId == dept)

  var depanddesg = (from b in db.TaskStatus

These translate to EXISTS / NOT EXISTS in SQL Server, which EF Core 10 supports, and preserve identical result sets. Apply the same four-query fix to the byte-identical SearchTaskStatus2 method (lines 547-598) since it shares the same break and is also still unfixed.
reasoning:
I read the flagged code (QuickNetCore/Controllers/TaskStatusController.cs, method SearchTaskStatus, lines 642-822) and the byte-identical legacy EF6 source (_archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/TaskStatusController.cs, lines 641-815). Confirmed the claim.

SERVER-SIDE: YES. The `depdesg` query at lines 740-748 is `from b in db.TaskStatus ...` â€” db.TaskStatus, db.TaskStatusDepts, db.TaskStatusDesgs are all real DbSets (ApplicationDbContext.cs lines 302, 449, 450), and there is NO .ToList()/.AsEnumerable() boundary before it. `depdesg` is therefore an un-materialized IQueryable<SelectFormat>. At line 749 it is composed via `full = full.Union(depdesg)`, where `full` is statically typed `IEnumerable<SelectFormat>` (declared line 738 as `new List<SelectFormat>()`). Overload resolution binds this to Enumerable.Union (LINQ-to-Objects), NOT Queryable.Union. But Enumerable.Union does NOT materialize depdesg first â€” when the final `.ToList()` (line 803/813, over `from b in full`) enumerates the union, Enumerable.Union calls GetEnumerator() on depdesg, which is still an EF IQueryable. That triggers EF Core's IQueryProvider.Execute and forces full SQL translation of the ENTIRE depdesg expression tree â€” including the `let chkdep = db.TaskStatusDepts.Where(...).Select(...).ToList()` / `let chkdesg = ....ToList()` and the `where chkdep.Count()==0 && chkdesg.Count()==0` predicate. The Enumerable.Union boundary does not rescue it.

WHY IT 500s: EF Core 10 cannot translate a `.ToList()` materialization embedded inside a query tree that is itself being translated to SQL (here, inside `let` bindings consumed by `where ... .Count()`). `.ToList()` is a terminal/materializing operator with no SQL node; the translator throws InvalidOperationException ("The LINQ expression ... could not be translated"). This is unhandled in the action and surfaces as HTTP 500. EF6 instead client-evaluated the predicate after materializing chkdep/chkdesg, so the legacy app worked â€” this is a pure port regression (legacy source is character-for-character identical at lines 740-792).

SEVERITY: HIGH. The entire else-branch (line 733+) runs whenever chkValue1==false, i.e. whenever the logged-in user is an employee with a department and/or designation â€” the common case. The depdesg query + full.Union(depdesg) + final .ToList() execute unconditionally in that branch; translation failure happens at execute time regardless of row count, so it 500s on every call, not just edge data.

CAVEAT on the claim's framing: the claim says this is the "same break class as SearchTaskStatus2 (line 548)" and implies that sibling was already fixed. I checked lines 547-598 and SearchTaskStatus2 is STILL in the original un-fixed `let ...ToList()/Count()==0/Contains()` form â€” neither method has been patched. This does not change the verdict for the flagged SearchTaskStatus location (it is genuinely broken); it just means the claim's "already-fixed sibling" premise is inaccurate, and the sibling needs the same fix too.

The siblings within SearchTaskStatus itself (dep @754-761, dessg @768-775, depanddesg @782-790) share the identical break (let ...ToList() + chkdep.Contains(dept)/chkdesg.Contains(desgn) over un-materialized db.TaskStatus consumed by Union+final .ToList()) and must be fixed together to fully resolve the method.

finalFix:
Rewrite all four query blocks in SearchleadStatus (TaskStatusController.cs) to drop the embedded `.ToList()` let bindings and use server-translatable .Any()/!.Any() predicates keyed on b.LeadStatusID, mirroring legacy output exactly.

Block 1 (lines 929-938, depdesg) â€” replace let+Count()==0 with negated Any:
var depdesg = (from b in db.LeadStatuss
               where !db.TaskStatusDepts.Any(a => a.TaskStatusId == b.LeadStatusID)
                  && !db.TaskStatusDesgs.Any(a => a.TaskStatusId == b.LeadStatusID)
                  && empid == true
               select new SelectFormat { text = b.StatusType, id = b.LeadStatusID });

Block 2 (lines 945-952, dep) â€” replace let+chkdep.Contains(dept) with Any(...&& DeptId==dept):
var dep = (from b in db.LeadStatuss
           where db.TaskStatusDepts.Any(a => a.TaskStatusId == b.LeadStatusID && a.DeptId == dept)
           select new SelectFormat { text = b.StatusType, id = b.LeadStatusID });

Block 3 (lines 959-966, dessg) â€” replace let+chkdesg.Contains(desgn) with Any(...&& DesgId==desgn):
var dessg = (from b in db.LeadStatuss
             where db.TaskStatusDesgs.Any(a => a.TaskStatusId == b.LeadStatusID && a.DesgId == desgn)
             select new SelectFormat { text = b.StatusType, id = b.LeadStatusID });

Block 4 (lines 973-981, depanddesg) â€” combine both Any predicates:
var depanddesg = (from b in db.LeadStatuss
                  where db.TaskStatusDepts.Any(a => a.TaskStatusId == b.LeadStatusID && a.DeptId == dept)
                     && db.TaskStatusDesgs.Any(a => a.TaskStatusId == b.LeadStatusID && a.DesgId == desgn)
                  select new SelectFormat { text = b.StatusType, id = b.LeadStatusID });

These are output-preserving: chkdep.Count()==0 â‡” !Any(matching dept rows); chkdep.Contains(dept) â‡” Any(row with that TaskStatusId AND DeptId==dept) â€” identical set semantics, now fully SQL-translatable (EXISTS/NOT EXISTS). DeptId/DeptId and DesgId are non-nullable long (ProTask.cs:381-392), and dept/desgn are non-nullable Int64 locals, so equality translates cleanly. All downstream code (full.Union, full.ToList, q-filtering, agnstat) is unchanged.

I traced every line from 170 to 232: there is NO .ToList()/.ToArray()/.AsEnumerable()/.AsAsyncEnumerable() boundary anywhere before the `select new {...}`. The first materialization is the `.ToList()` at line 232. Confirmed via Grep that the only .AsEnumerable() calls in this file are in OTHER methods (lines 377, 1853, 1890, 2271) â€” GetHireReturn has none. So the `let app/AppStatus/chkAppStatus` (lines 185-189) feed a server-translated projection (app=app, AppStatus=AppStatus, chkAppStatus=chkAppStatus at lines 226-228). Embedded as a correlated collection in the outer unmaterialized projection, this surfaces as an HTTP 500 at line 232 enumeration. Translation fails BEFORE row evaluation, so it 500s on the FIRST request regardless of data (even empty ApprovalUpdates) â€” hence high, not edge-only. (The trailing .ToList().Select(...).ToList() does NOT create a client boundary because the whole expression is still inside the outer IQueryable's expression tree.) The `app` and `AppStatus` scalar collection subqueries are translatable on their own; chkAppStatus is the guaranteed breaker.

Inspected QuickNetCore/Controllers/CrossHireReturnController.cs GetCrossHireReturn (the [HttpPost] DataTables grid loader, lines 169-275). The outer query (var v = from b in db.CrossHireReturns join db.* ... select new {...}) is genuinely SERVER-SIDE: there is no .AsEnumerable()/.ToList() boundary anywhere before the select new; the first materialization is .ToList() at line 228, AFTER the projection. Triaging each `let`:
- app (184) = db.Approvals.Where(correlated).Select(x=>x.EmployeeId).ToList() and AppStatus (185) = same shape: these are plain correlated COLLECTION projections (List<scalar>), which EF Core 3.0+ translates natively as correlated subqueries. - chkAppStatus (186-188) = db.ApprovalUpdates.Where(correlated).GroupBy(l=>l.ApprovedBy).Select(g=>g.OrderByDescending(c=>c.CreatedDate).FirstOrDefault()).ToList().Select(x=>x.ApprovalStatus).ToList(). DECISIVE corroboration (Grep across live tree): the codebase maintainers already established the exact fix for this exact expression. CrossHireReturnController:186 lacks that .AsEnumerable() and is an un-fixed instance of the identical class (as is the sibling HireReturnController:187 and a few others). 

Port regression confirmed: _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/CrossHireReturnController.cs lines 180-182 are byte-identical to the ported block minus .AsEnumerable(); EF6's LINQ-to-Entities client-evaluated this GroupBy/FirstOrDefault, EF Core 10 does not. Bottom line: the hunter's conclusion (method 500s, fix = add .AsEnumerable() before the chkAppStatus GroupBy) is correct, even though app/AppStatus by themselves are translatable. Confirmed.

    var saleitem = (from b in db.MaterialRequisitionItems
                    join c in db.Items on b.Item equals c.ItemID
                    join d in db.Scaffolds on c.ItemID equals d.Item into scaffold
                    from d in scaffold.DefaultIfEmpty()
                    join e in db.ItemUnits on b.ItemUnit equals e.ItemUnitID into punit
                    from e in punit.DefaultIfEmpty()
                    join g in db.ItemBundles on c.ItemID equals g.mainItem into bundle
                    from g in bundle.DefaultIfEmpty()
                    join h in db.ItemBrands on b.Make equals h.ItemBrandID into brn
                    from h in brn.DefaultIfEmpty()
                    where b.MaterialRequisition == id && b.ItemNote != "-:{Bundle_Item}"
                    select new { b, c, d, e, h })
                   .AsEnumerable()
                   .Select(x => new {
                       ItemNote = x.b.ItemNote,
                       ItemQuantity = x.b.ItemQuantity,
                       ItemCode = x.c.ItemCode,
                       ItemName = x.c.ItemName,
                       ItemUnit = x.e.ItemUnitName,
                       PartNumber = x.c.PartNumber,
                       ItemRemark = x.b.ItemRemark,
                       CBM = x.d.CBM,
                       Weight = x.d.Weight,
                       img = db.ItemImages.Where(im => im.ItemID == x.b.Item).Select(im => new pdfItemImg { FileName = im.FileName, Status = im.Status, ItemImageID = im.ItemImageID }).ToList(),
                       ItemDescription = x.c.ItemDescription,
                       KeepStock = x.c.KeepStock,
                       Make = x.h.ItemBrandName,

                                 from eb in bpunit.DefaultIfEmpty()
                                 where az.mainItem == x.b.Item
                                 select new {
                                     Id = bb.ItemID,
                                     ItemQuantity = (ay.ItemQuantity * x.b.ItemQuantity),
                                     ItemNote = "",
                                     ItemCode = bb.ItemCode,
                                     ItemName = bb.ItemName,
                                     ItemUnit = eb.ItemUnitName,
                                     PartNumber = bb.PartNumber,
                                     CBM = dd.CBM,
                                     Weight = dd.Weight,

finalFix:
Mirror EF6's client-side evaluation by introducing an `.AsEnumerable()` materialization boundary BEFORE the `let` block, in BOTH copies (the GetPurchaseEntryPayment-family register grids at lines ~3050 and ~3274). Concretely, split the single LINQ query so the join+filter base runs server-side and the `let app/AppStatus/chkAppStatus/chkAppStatusme/sh` subqueries + final `select new {...}` run client-side:

1. Build the base query from `from a in db.PurchaseEntrys join ... from h in hir.DefaultIfEmpty()` through the `where ...` clause, projecting only the entity rows needed (e.g. `select new { a, b, c, d, g, i, h }`).
2. Apply `.AsEnumerable()` (or `.ToList()`) to that base query to cross the materialization boundary â€” exactly as done in the Payment/HireReturn fixes elsewhere in this audit.
3. Then run the `let app = db.AssignedTos...ToList()`, `let AppStatus = db.ApprovalUpdates...ToList()`, and especially `let chkAppStatus = db.ApprovalUpdates...GroupBy(...).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(...).FirstOrDefault()).ToList().Select(x => x.ApprovalStatus).ToList()` and the final `select new {...}` over the materialized base sequence so they evaluate in-memory (LINQ-to-Objects), where GroupBy-first-per-group is fully supported.

This preserves identical output (each chkAppStatus subquery still keys by a.PurchaseEntryId / each row's PurchaseEntryId) while moving the untranslatable GroupBy projection client-side. Apply identically at line 3062 (GetPurchaseEntryPayment) and line 3286 (the second grid). Note these subqueries fire per base row (N+1) after the change â€” acceptable and matches EF6 behavior; if perf matters, the GroupBy results can be pre-fetched into a Dictionary keyed by (TransEntry) after the base materialization, but that is an optional optimization, not required for correctness.

(a) SERVER-SIDE: The flagged `let` block (lines 3062-3068 first copy / 3286-3292 second copy) is inside `var v = (from a in db.PurchaseEntrys join ... select new {...}).ToList()...`. `db.PurchaseEntrys`, `db.ApprovalUpdates`, `db.AssignedTos` are real EF Core DbSets on ApplicationDbContext (ApplicationDbContext.cs:125/355/372). There is NO `.AsEnumerable()`/`.ToList()` boundary before the `select new {...}`; the FIRST materialization is the `.ToList()` at line 3125 (copy 1) / 3349 (copy 2). So the entire projection â€” app, AppStatus, and chkAppStatus â€” is composed into one server-side IQueryable and translated to SQL at that `.ToList()`. The `let` fragments are inside the boundary, not after it. Confirmed server-side. (The line-3286 copy's extra `where approveemp.Contains(empl.EmployeeId)` uses pre-computed C# locals, a constant boolean, and does not change translation.)

(b) WOULD 500: The decisive fragment is chkAppStatus: `db.ApprovalUpdates.Where(x => x.TransEntry==a.PurchaseEntryId && x.Type=="PurchaseEntryPayment").GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(c => c.CreatedDate).FirstOrDefault()).ToList().Select(x => x.ApprovalStatus).ToList()`. Both line-3062 and line-3286 copies are live (non-commented, reachable, return Json data at lines 3177/3401). The hunter's claim is validated.

I grepped the whole method â€” the only `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()` boundaries are the TRAILING ones at line 244 (amc) and 267 (task). No materialization precedes them, so the `let assign = subquery.ToList()` and its `assign.Contains(EmpId)` consumption in the `where` are composed into the un-materialized IQueryable. EmployeeId is `long` (non-nullable) on both PeriodicMaintAssignedTo (Models/AMC.cs:216) and TaskAssigned (Models/ProTask.cs:325); EmpId is `long` (db.Employees...Select(EmployeeId).FirstOrDefault()), so `assign` is `List<long>` and the predicate is `List<long>.Contains(long)`.

So the `let` cannot be blindly dropped there; only the WHERE consumption (line 253) must change to `.Any()` EXISTS, leaving the `let` for the terminal projection. In the `amc` query, `assign` is used only in the where (line 231), so its `let` can be dropped entirely.

why: Identical server-side 2-level nested-collection projection over db.SalesOrderItems (img let-bound collection + bundle inline collection containing bimg). EF Core 10 cannot translate -> 500 on Sales Order print.
finalFix:
Mirror the legacy EF6 output by moving the nested collections out of the server-side projection (exactly the safe `from o in v` idiom already used at Common.cs:12435/12660/12859):

1. Materialize the flat SCALAR rows first (no img, no bundle) into a List:
   var rows = (from b in db.SalesOrderItems
               join c in db.Items on b.Item equals c.ItemID
               join d in db.Scaffolds on c.ItemID equals d.Item into scaffold from d in scaffold.DefaultIfEmpty()
               join e in db.ItemUnits on b.ItemUnit equals e.ItemUnitID into punit from e in punit.DefaultIfEmpty()
               where b.SalesOrder == id && b.ItemNote != "-:{Bundle_Item}"
               select new pdfItemViewModel {
                   ItemUnitPrice = b.ItemUnitPrice, ItemQuantity = b.ItemQuantity, ItemSubTotal = b.ItemSubTotal,
                   ItemTax = b.ItemTax, ItemNote = b.ItemNote, ItemTaxAmount = b.ItemTaxAmount,
                   ItemTotalAmount = b.ItemTotalAmount, ItemDiscount = b.ItemDiscount, ItemCode = c.ItemCode,
                   ItemName = c.ItemName, ItemUnit = e.ItemUnitName, PartNumber = c.PartNumber, PNoStatus = PNoStatus,
                   CBM = d.CBM, Weight = d.Weight, ItemDescription = c.ItemDescription, KeepStock = c.KeepStock,
                   Id = b.Item   // keep the item id so we can attach images/bundle below
               }).ToList();

2. After materialization, attach img and bundle client-side per row (each db.* call now runs as its own query against the in-memory list, exactly like EF6's per-row behavior):
   foreach (var r in rows) {
       r.img = db.ItemImages.Where(im => im.ItemID == r.Id)
                 .Select(im => new pdfItemImg { FileName = im.FileName, Status = im.Status, ItemImageID = im.ItemImageID }).ToList();
       r.bundle = (from ay in db.BundleItems
                   join az in db.ItemBundles on ay.ItemBundle equals az.ItemBundleId
                   join bb in db.Items on ay.ItemId equals bb.ItemID
                   join dd in db.Scaffolds on bb.ItemID equals dd.Item into scaffold from dd in scaffold.DefaultIfEmpty()
                   join eb in db.ItemUnits on ay.ItemUnit equals eb.ItemUnitID into bpunit from eb in bpunit.DefaultIfEmpty()
                   where az.mainItem == r.Id
                   select new pdfBundleViewModel {
                       Id = bb.ItemID, ItemUnitPrice = ay.ItemUnitPrice,
                       ItemQuantity = ay.ItemQuantity * r.ItemQuantity,
                       ItemSubTotal = ay.ItemQuantity * r.ItemQuantity * ay.ItemUnitPrice,
                       ItemNote = "", ItemTax = ay.ItemTax,
                       ItemTaxAmount = (ay.ItemQuantity * r.ItemQuantity * ay.ItemUnitPrice) * ay.ItemTax / 100,
                       ItemTotalAmount = ay.ItemTotalAmount, ItemCode = bb.ItemCode, ItemName = bb.ItemName,
                       ItemUnit = eb.ItemUnitName, PartNumber = bb.PartNumber, PNoStatus = PNoStatus,
                       CBM = dd.CBM, Weight = dd.Weight, KeepStock = bb.KeepStock, Item = ay.ItemId, ItemDiscount = 0,
                       ItemWithCode = bb.ItemCode + " - " + bb.ItemName, ItemUnitID = bb.ItemUnitID, SubUnitId = bb.SubUnitId,
                       ItemArabic = bb.ItemArabic, ItemDescription = bb.ItemDescription
                   }).ToList();           // bundle still materialized per row
       foreach (var bn in r.bundle)
           bn.img = db.ItemImages.Where(im => im.ItemID == bn.Item)
                      .Select(im => new pdfItemImg { FileName = im.FileName, Status = im.Status, ItemImageID = im.ItemImageID }).ToList();
   }
   vmodel.pdfItem = rows;

This preserves the legacy EF6 result set exactly (the bundle quantity/amount math uses r.ItemQuantity, matching the original b.ItemQuantity) while eliminating the un-translatable 2-level nested collection projection. Apply the identical treatment to the sibling SaleData (Common.cs:8538, mobmodel/settlment inline `.ToList()`s) and the other direct-from-DbSet pdfItem builders (QuotationData @10043, WItems @10220, etc.) that share the same nested-collection shape.

Minimal output-preserving rewrite of QuickNetCore/Models/Common.cs lines 10043-10117:

1. In the OUTER server query, drop the nested `img = img` and `bundle = (...).ToList()` members and the `let img = ...` (remove img/bundle from the projected pdfItemViewModel). End the outer query with `.AsEnumerable()` (or `.ToList()`).

2. Then a client-side `.Select(o => new pdfItemViewModel { ...copy o's scalars..., img = ..., bundle = ... }).ToList()`:
   - img = db.ItemImages.Where(im => im.ItemID == o.ItemKey).Select(im => new pdfItemImg { FileName = im.FileName, Status = im.Status, ItemImageID = im.ItemImageID }).ToList();
   - bundle = (from ay in db.BundleItems
              join az in db.ItemBundles on ay.ItemBundle equals az.ItemBundleId
              join bb in db.Items on ay.ItemId equals bb.ItemID
              join dd in db.Scaffolds on bb.ItemID equals dd.Item into scaffold from dd in scaffold.DefaultIfEmpty()
              join eb in db.ItemUnits on ay.ItemUnit equals eb.ItemUnitID into bpunit from eb in bpunit.DefaultIfEmpty()
              where az.mainItem == o.ItemKey
              select new { ay, bb, dd, eb }).AsEnumerable()
             .Select(x => new pdfBundleViewModel {
                 ... all the existing scalar bundle members (note ItemQuantity/ItemSubTotal/ItemTaxAmount multiply by o.ItemQuantity, preserved via captured outer qty) ...,
                 img = db.ItemImages.Where(bim => bim.ItemID == x.ay.ItemId).Select(bim => new pdfItemImg { FileName = bim.FileName, Status = bim.Status, ItemImageID = bim.ItemImageID }).ToList()
             }).ToList();

Because the bundle multiplications use the outer `b.ItemQuantity`, also capture `OuterQty = b.ItemQuantity` in step 1 and use it in the bundle math (replace `b.ItemQuantity` with `o.OuterQty`). Each subquery now runs as its own client-side query exactly like EF6's per-row behavior, and the final List<pdfItemViewModel> (with populated img + bundle + inner bundle.img) is byte-identical to the legacy output. No server-side nested-collection projection remains, so EF Core 10 translates each fragment and the 500 is eliminated.

Mirror the legacy N+1 behavior by stripping the nested collections out of the EF projection, materializing the flat rows, then attaching img/bundle via client-side lookups. Output is preserved exactly.

1) Outer query â€” remove the `let img = ...` and replace the inline `bundle = (...).ToList()` and `img = img` with placeholders, materialize, then fill in per row:

vmodel.pdfItem = (from b in db.PFItemss
                  join c in db.Items on b.Item equals c.ItemID
                  join d in db.Scaffolds on c.ItemID equals d.Item into scaffold
                  from d in scaffold.DefaultIfEmpty()
                  join e in db.ItemUnits on b.ItemUnit equals e.ItemUnitID into punit
                  from e in punit.DefaultIfEmpty()
                  join g in db.ItemBundles on c.ItemID equals g.mainItem into bundle
                  from g in bundle.DefaultIfEmpty()
                  where b.ProForma == id && b.itemNote != "-:{Bundle_Item}"
                  select new pdfItemViewModel
                  {
                      Id = c.ItemID,
                      ItemUnitPrice = b.ItemUnitPrice,
                      ItemQuantity = b.ItemQuantity,
                      ItemSubTotal = b.ItemSubTotal,
                      ItemNote = b.itemNote,
                      ItemTax = b.ItemTax,
                      ItemTaxAmount = b.ItemTaxAmount,
                      ItemTotalAmount = b.ItemTotalAmount,
                      ItemDiscount = b.ItemDiscount,
                      ItemCode = c.ItemCode,
                      ItemName = c.ItemName,
                      ItemUnit = e.ItemUnitName,
                      PartNumber = c.PartNumber,
                      InSaleInvoice = c.InSaleInvoice,
                      PNoStatus = PNoStatus,
                      CBM = d.CBM,
                      Weight = d.Weight,
                      // img attached client-side below
                      ItemDescription = c.ItemDescription,
                      KeepStock = c.KeepStock,
                      // bundle attached client-side below
                  }).ToList();   // <-- materialization boundary

// client-side fill (now N+1, exactly like EF6):
foreach (var it in vmodel.pdfItem)
{
    it.img = db.ItemImages
        .Where(im => im.ItemID == it.Id)
        .Select(im => new pdfItemImg { FileName = im.FileName, Status = im.Status, ItemImageID = im.ItemImageID })
        .ToList();

    it.bundle = (from ay in db.BundleItems
                 join az in db.ItemBundles on ay.ItemBundle equals az.ItemBundleId
                 join bb in db.Items on ay.ItemId equals bb.ItemID
                 join dd in db.Scaffolds on bb.ItemID equals dd.Item into scaffold
                 from dd in scaffold.DefaultIfEmpty()
                 join eb in db.ItemUnits on ay.ItemUnit equals eb.ItemUnitID into bpunit
                 from eb in bpunit.DefaultIfEmpty()
                 where az.mainItem == it.Id
                 select new pdfBundleViewModel
                 {
                     Id = bb.ItemID,
                     ItemUnitPrice = ay.ItemUnitPrice,
                     ItemQuantity = (ay.ItemQuantity * it.ItemQuantity),
                     ItemSubTotal = (ay.ItemQuantity * it.ItemQuantity * ay.ItemUnitPrice),
                     ItemNote = "",
                     ItemTax = ay.ItemTax,
                     ItemTaxAmount = ((ay.ItemQuantity * it.ItemQuantity * ay.ItemUnitPrice) * ay.ItemTax / 100),
                     ItemTotalAmount = ay.ItemTotalAmount,
                     ItemCode = bb.ItemCode,
                     ItemName = bb.ItemName,
                     ItemUnit = eb.ItemUnitName,
                     PartNumber = bb.PartNumber,
                     PNoStatus = PNoStatus,
                     CBM = dd.CBM,
                     Weight = dd.Weight,
                     // img attached client-side below
                     KeepStock = bb.KeepStock,
                     Item = ay.ItemId,
                     ItemDiscount = 0,
                     ItemWithCode = bb.ItemCode + " - " + bb.ItemName,
                     ItemUnitID = bb.ItemUnitID,
                     SubUnitId = bb.SubUnitId,
                     ItemArabic = bb.ItemArabic,
                     ItemDescription = bb.ItemDescription
                 }).ToList();   // <-- materialization boundary

    foreach (var bd in it.bundle)
    {
        bd.img = db.ItemImages
            .Where(bim => bim.ItemID == bd.Item)
            .Select(bim => new pdfItemImg { FileName = bim.FileName, Status = bim.Status, ItemImageID = bim.ItemImageID })
            .ToList();
    }
}

Notes preserving exact legacy semantics:
- The original `bundle` sub-query referenced the OUTER row's `b.Item` (in `where az.mainItem == b.Item`) and `b.ItemQuantity` (in the quantity math). After moving client-side, use `it.Id` for `b.Item` (Id = c.ItemID and the join is c.ItemID == g.mainItem, and the where used b.Item which equals c.ItemID via the join) and `it.ItemQuantity` for `b.ItemQuantity`. If b.Item could differ from c.ItemID in data, capture b.Item into the outer projection (add a field) and use that captured value instead of it.Id â€” safest is to add `Item = b.Item` to pdfItemViewModel and key bundle off it.
- `img = img` originally keyed on `b.Item`; client-side keep keying on the same item id (it.Id / captured b.Item).
- Apply the identical transformation to the sibling builders in the same systemic class (PurchaseQuotationData at ~10840 and any other Common.cs PDF builders with the img+bundle+bimg shape) for consistency, per prior batches.

why: Three sibling server-side branches (DummyPEItems2 @11490, PEItemss @11571, PEItemss @11653) all project the img (let-bound) + bundle (inline, with inner bimg) collections. EF Core 10 cannot translate any of them -> 500 on Purchase Entry print regardless of the StockTransferUpdate branch taken.
finalFix:
Apply the SAME materialize-then-attach fix to ALL THREE branches (Common.cs ~11490, ~11571, ~11653), mirroring the legacy EF6 result. For each branch:

1) Reduce the OUTER query to scalar columns only (drop `img = img` and the inline `bundle = (...).ToList()` from the `select new pdfItemViewModel`, and drop the `let img = ...` line), then `.ToList()` to force client-side enumeration. Keep all scalar assignments (Id, ItemUnitPrice, ItemQuantity, ItemCode, ItemName, CBM, Weight, etc.) and carry `Id = b.ItemID` plus `Item`/`ItemDiscount` so the later lookups can correlate.

2) After materializing the outer list, populate `img` per row from a client-side lookup:
   `foreach (var row in vmodel.pdfItem) row.img = db.ItemImages.Where(im => im.ItemID == row.Id).Select(im => new pdfItemImg { FileName = im.FileName, Status = im.Status, ItemImageID = im.ItemImageID }).ToList();`

3) Populate `bundle` per row with the bundle subquery moved to top-level (now a standalone server-side query, which IS translatable), but with ITS inner `bimg` collection also materialized-then-attached the same way. Concretely, for each outer row (row.Id == b.ItemID), run:
   `var bundleRows = (from ab in db.PEItemss join bb in db.Items on ab.Item equals bb.ItemID join dd in db.Scaffolds on bb.ItemID equals dd.Item into sc from dd in sc.DefaultIfEmpty() join eb in db.ItemUnits on ab.ItemUnit equals eb.ItemUnitID into bu from eb in bu.DefaultIfEmpty() where ab.PurchaseEntry == id && ab.itemNote == "-:{Bundle_Item}" && ab.ItemDiscount == row.Id select new pdfBundleViewModel { Id = bb.ItemID, ItemUnitPrice = ab.ItemUnitPrice, ItemQuantity = ab.ItemQuantity, ItemSubTotal = ab.ItemSubTotal, ItemNote = "", ItemTax = ab.ItemTax, ItemTaxAmount = ab.ItemTaxAmount, ItemTotalAmount = ab.ItemTotalAmount, ItemCode = bb.ItemCode, ItemName = bb.ItemName, ItemUnit = eb.ItemUnitName, PartNumber = bb.PartNumber, PNoStatus = PNoStatus, CBM = dd.CBM, Weight = dd.Weight, KeepStock = bb.KeepStock, Item = ab.Item, ItemDiscount = 0, ItemWithCode = bb.ItemCode + " - " + bb.ItemName, ItemUnitID = bb.ItemUnitID, SubUnitId = bb.SubUnitId, ItemArabic = bb.ItemArabic, ItemDescription = bb.ItemDescription }).ToList();`
   Then attach each bundle row's image collection client-side:
   `foreach (var br in bundleRows) br.img = db.ItemImages.Where(bim => bim.ItemID == br.Item).Select(bim => new pdfItemImg { FileName = bim.FileName, Status = bim.Status, ItemImageID = bim.ItemImageID }).ToList();`
   `row.bundle = bundleRows;`

This preserves the exact legacy output (same rows, same img/bundle/bimg contents, same `b.ItemID == ab.ItemDiscount` correlation) while eliminating the nested-correlated-collection-in-server-projection that EF Core 10 cannot translate. The bundle correlation `b.ItemID == ab.ItemDiscount` maps to `ab.ItemDiscount == row.Id` since `row.Id = b.ItemID` in the outer projection. Note this is an N+1 query pattern (acceptable for a single-document print path, and is exactly how EF6 behaved). Apply identically to the DummyPEItems2 branch (11490) and both PEItemss branches (11571, 11653).

why: Server-side query over db.ProcessFlows (Task process-flow config grid) with two inline nested .ToList() collection projections materialized at line 100. EF Core 10 cannot translate -> 500 on the Task Process Flow master grid.
finalFix:
In QuickNetCore/Controllers/ProcessFlowController.cs, GetProcessFlow, split the query so the nested collections are loaded client-side via bulk lookups (mirrors EF6 per-row behavior, identical output):

1) Materialize the scalar rows first (no nested collections in the server-side projection):
```
var baseRows = (from a in db.ProcessFlows
                join b in db.TaskStatus on a.TaskStatus equals b.TaskStatusId into tmp
                from b in tmp.DefaultIfEmpty()
                select new {
                    a.ProcessFlowId,
                    a.RemoveUpdateUser,
                    a.RemoveUpdateUserTeams,
                    TaskStatus = b.StatusName,
                }).ToList();

var ids = baseRows.Select(r => r.ProcessFlowId).ToList();
```

2) Pull the two child collections in two bulk server-side queries (each is now a flat projection EF Core 10 can translate), keyed by ProcessFlowId, materialized with .ToList(), then grouped in memory:
```
var assignTypeLookup = (from z in db.ProcessFlowAssignTypes
                        join w in db.Teams on z.TeamId equals w.TeamId
                        join y in db.Employees on w.TeamLead equals y.EmployeeId
                        where ids.Contains(z.ProcessFlowId)
                        select new { z.ProcessFlowId, w.TeamName })
                       .ToList()
                       .GroupBy(x => x.ProcessFlowId)
                       .ToDictionary(g => g.Key, g => g.Select(x => new { x.TeamName }).ToList());

var assignedUsersLookup = (from z in db.ProcessFlowAssignUsers
                           join y in db.Employees on z.EmployeeId equals y.EmployeeId
                           where ids.Contains(z.ProcessFlowId)
                           select new {
                               z.ProcessFlowId,
                               id = y.EmployeeId,
                               LastName = (y.LastName != null) ? y.LastName : "",
                               FirstName = (y.FirstName != null) ? y.FirstName : "",
                               MiddleName = (y.MiddleName != null) ? y.MiddleName : "",
                               Img = (y.ImgFileName != null) ? y.ImgFileName : "",
                               y.Status
                           })
                          .ToList()
                          .GroupBy(x => x.ProcessFlowId)
                          .ToDictionary(g => g.Key, g => g.Select(x => new { x.id, x.LastName, x.FirstName, x.MiddleName, x.Img, x.Status }).ToList());
```

3) Build the final client-side shape (same fields/order as the original `.ToList().Select(o => new {...})`):
```
var UserView = baseRows.Select(o => new {
    id = o.ProcessFlowId,
    AssignType = assignTypeLookup.TryGetValue(o.ProcessFlowId, out var at) ? at : new List<dynamic>().Select(x=>new{TeamName=(string)null}).ToList(),
    o.RemoveUpdateUser,
    o.RemoveUpdateUserTeams,
    AssignedUsers = assignedUsersLookup.TryGetValue(o.ProcessFlowId, out var au) ? au : null,
    o.TaskStatus,
});
```
(Use empty lists rather than null for the missing-key case to exactly match the EF6 output where the inner .ToList() returned an empty list; adjust the default to `new List<...>()` of the matching anonymous type for compile-time typing.) The rest of the method (search filter on TaskStatus, dynamic OrderBy via .AsQueryable(), Count, Skip/Take, Json) is unchanged and operates over the in-memory IEnumerable as before.

Mirror EF6 by materializing the outer scalars first, then building the two sub-collections from bulk lookups keyed by LeadProcessFlowId (preserves identical JSON shape/order). Replace the query at lines 61-99 with:

var baseRows = (from a in db.LeadProcessFlows
                join b in db.LeadStatuss on a.LeadStatus equals b.LeadStatusID into tmp
                from b in tmp.DefaultIfEmpty()
                select new
                {
                    a.LeadProcessFlowId,

                    a.RemoveUpdateUserTeams,
                    a.LeadStatus,
                    StatusType = b != null ? b.StatusType : null,
                }).ToList();

var ids = baseRows.Select(r => r.LeadProcessFlowId).ToList();

// AssignType lookup (TeamName per LeadProcessFlowId) â€” mirrors nested join Teams+Employees
var assignTypeLookup = (from z in db.LeadProcessFlowAssignTypes
                        join w in db.Teams on z.TeamId equals w.TeamId
                        join y in db.Employees on w.TeamLead equals y.EmployeeId

   var task = (from a in db.ProTasks.Where(o => o.Branch == 1)
               join e in db.Employees on a.driver equals e.EmployeeId
               where (EF.Functions.DateDiffMinute(a.StartTime, fdate) >= 0) &&
                     (EF.Functions.DateDiffMinute(a.EndTime, fdate) <= 0) &&
                     a.driver == driver
               select new { a.ProTaskId, a.StartTime, a.EndTime, a.TaskName, e.FirstName }).ToList();

2) Build the assignee names once (place it before the loop, keyed by ProTaskId). This reproduces the legacy `(join Employees ... where Status=="Assigned" && chkStatus==active).Distinct().Select(FirstName)`:

   var assignedLookup = (from z in db.TaskAssigneds
                         join y in db.Employees on z.EmployeeId equals y.EmployeeId
                         where z.Status == "Assigned" && z.chkStatus == Status.active
                         select new { z.ProTaskId, FirstName = (y.FirstName != null) ? y.FirstName : "" })
                        .Distinct().ToList()
                        .GroupBy(x => x.ProTaskId)
                        .ToDictionary(g => g.Key, g => String.Join(",", g.Select(o => o.FirstName).ToArray()));

   (db.TaskAssigneds.Join(...).Where(...).Distinct().ToList() is itself a top-level standalone query, so it materializes safely; the GroupBy/ToDictionary then runs client-side.)

This preserves the exact distinct, comma-joined first-name string (AssignedTo is used for nothing else) while eliminating the untranslatable nested collection projection. Note: this is the only behavior `cald` derives from AssignedTo, so no other call sites are affected.

finalFix:
In Models/Common.cs, method SalesOrderData, replace the L9756 line:

    validity = (DateTime.Now <= DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(b.SODate, (b.SOValidity == null) ? 0 : b.SOValidity + 1)) ? "Active" : "Expired",

with the EF-native, DATEADD-translatable form (mirrors legacy DbFunctions.AddDays semantics; SODate is non-nullable DateTime so use it directly, no .Value):

    validity = (b.SODate.AddDays((b.SOValidity == null) ? 0 : (b.SOValidity.Value + 1)) >= DateTime.Now) ? "Active" : "Expired",

Rationale: EF Core 10 translates `DateTime.AddDays(int)` on a mapped column to SQL DATEADD, so it stays server-side and preserves the legacy result. `DateTime.Now <= AddDays(x)` is logically equivalent to `AddDays(x) >= DateTime.Now`, so output is unchanged. The days argument matches legacy: 0 when SOValidity is null, else SOValidity+1. (If you prefer to keep the exact original comparison direction, `DateTime.Now <= b.SODate.AddDays(...)` also translates and works.)

method: GetReport(string user, string From, string To)
breakClass: GroupBy-then-Select(g => g.OrderBy*/FirstOrDefault()) returning an ENTITY/row, used server-side (JOINed on g.FirstOrDefault(), or composed as a correlated 'latest-per-key' subquery in a non-materialized IQueryable). EF Core 10 cannot translate the grouped FirstOrDefault outside an aggregate context -> "could not be translated" runtime 500. Every finding has the identical shape in _archive/legacy-mvc5 where EF6 translated it, so each is a port regression.
why: Canonical break class. group a by EmployeeName into g, then join on g.FirstOrDefault().EmployeeName and select g.FirstOrDefault().Id. v is a pure IQueryable enumerated only at v.Count()/v.ToList() (no AsEnumerable/ToList boundary), so server-side. EF Core 10 cannot translate a grouping element's g.FirstOrDefault() (non-aggregate over IGrouping) used as a JOIN key/SELECT -> 'LINQ expression could not be translated' => 500 on the EmployeeAttendance report grid whenever attendance rows exist. Legacy has the identical query => port regression.
finalFix:
In QuickNetCore/Controllers/EmployeeAttendanceController.cs, GetReport, replace the untranslatable grouped-FirstOrDefault query (lines 567-581) with a Users-driven query that uses a correlated Any() filter and a correlated FirstOrDefault() for id (preserves "one row per employee-user with attendance in window", id = first attendance Id, user = UserName):

var v = (from c in db.Users
         where (from a in db.EmpAttendances
                where a.EmployeeName == c.Id
                  && (From == null || From == "" || EF.Functions.DateDiffDay(a.login, crdate) <= 0)
                  && (To == null || To == "" || EF.Functions.DateDiffDay(a.login, exdate) >= 0)
                select a).Any()
            && (user == null || user == "" || c.Id == user)
         select new
         {
             id = (from a in db.EmpAttendances
                   where a.EmployeeName == c.Id
                     && (From == null || From == "" || EF.Functions.DateDiffDay(a.login, crdate) <= 0)
                     && (To == null || To == "" || EF.Functions.DateDiffDay(a.login, exdate) >= 0)
                   select (long?)a.Id).FirstOrDefault(),
             user = c.UserName,
         });

This keeps `v` fully server-side (translates to EXISTS + a correlated TOP(1) subquery, both supported by EF Core 10), preserves the existing date filters, the (user==null||user==""||c.Id==user) filter, and the downstream search (p.id...) / Distinct().OrderBy / Count() / Skip().Take().ToList() unchanged. `id` becomes long? â€” harmless since the grid only ToString()s it; cast to (long) on the projection if a non-nullable is required. Alternatively, materialize first: var names = db.EmpAttendances.Where(<date filters>).Select(a=>a.EmployeeName).Distinct().ToList(); then join db.Users client-side â€” but the correlated form above mirrors legacy output most faithfully and avoids loading rows.
reasoning:
I independently confirmed the hunter's claim against the live file QuickNetCore/Controllers/EmployeeAttendanceController.cs, method GetReport(string user, string From, string To), lines 567-597.

SERVER-SIDE: confirmed. `db` is the real ApplicationDbContext (field decl line 33). `db.EmpAttendances` is a server DbSet (ApplicationDbContext.cs line 515) and `db.Users` is the Identity server DbSet (ApplicationDbContext : IdentityDbContext<ApplicationUser>). `v` (lines 567-581) is a pure IQueryable composed via `from a in db.EmpAttendances ... group a by a.EmployeeName into g join c in db.Users on g.FirstOrDefault().EmployeeName equals c.Id ... select new { id = g.FirstOrDefault().Id, user = c.UserName }`. Between definition and first enumeration there is NO .ToList()/.ToArray()/.AsEnumerable()/.AsAsyncEnumerable() boundary â€” only further IQueryable composition (.Where at 587, .Distinct().OrderBy at 594). First enumeration is `v.Count()` (line 596) and `v.Skip(skip).Take(pageSize).ToList()` (line 597). So the grouped FirstOrDefault runs server-side. It is NOT a safe group-aggregate-over-IGrouping case: `g.FirstOrDefault()` returns a whole entity (then .EmployeeName / .Id), not an aggregate, and it is used as a JOIN key and a scalar projection.

WOULD 500: yes. EF Core 3.0+/10 only translates GroupBy that terminates in key + aggregate functions. A non-aggregate `g.FirstOrDefault()` element access used as a join key and projection is untranslatable; EF Core throws InvalidOperationException ("The LINQ expression ... could not be translated") at query evaluation (v.Count()). EF Core 3.0+ removed implicit client evaluation of arbitrary fragments, and Program.cs (lines 35-36) registers the context with no client-eval warning override, so there is no fallback. The throw happens at translation time, so it 500s on every invocation regardless of data (even empty table) â€” severity HIGH.

PORT REGRESSION: yes. The legacy EF6 source at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/EmployeeAttendanceController.cs, method GetReport lines 595-610, is byte-identical except `DbFunctions.DiffDays` vs `EF.Functions.DateDiffDay`; EF6 translated the grouped FirstOrDefault. Same shape, same enumeration boundary (v.Count() line 625, v.Skip().Take().ToList() line 626). So this is a pure port regression introduced by moving to EF Core 10.

Semantics to preserve: legacy keys attendance to users via EmpAttendance.EmployeeName == ApplicationUser.Id; output is one row per employee-user having attendance in the [From,To] window, with id = the (arbitrary first) EmpAttendance.Id for that employee and user = UserName.

method: GetReport(string user, string From, string To)
breakClass: GroupBy-then-Select(g => g.OrderBy*/FirstOrDefault()) returning an ENTITY/row, used server-side (JOINed on g.FirstOrDefault(), or composed as a correlated 'latest-per-key' subquery in a non-materialized IQueryable). EF Core 10 cannot translate the grouped FirstOrDefault outside an aggregate context -> "could not be translated" runtime 500. Every finding has the identical shape in _archive/legacy-mvc5 where EF6 translated it, so each is a port regression.
why: Third copy of the same GetReport (EmpAttentanceReport grid). Same grouped-FirstOrDefault join, server-side (only enumerated at v.Count()/v.ToList()). EF Core 10 untranslatable -> 500. Legacy _archive/legacy-mvc5/.../ShowRoomItemForecastController.cs:1293 has identical 'group a by a.EmployeeName into g' => port regression.
finalFix:
Replace the grouped-FirstOrDefault join and projection with translatable per-group aggregates that mirror the legacy intent (one representative attendance row per EmployeeName, joined to its User by Id). Since g.FirstOrDefault().EmployeeName is just the grouping key and g.FirstOrDefault().Id is the representative attendance Id, use g.Key for the join key and g.Max(x => x.Id) (or g.Min) for the id:

var v = (from a in db.EmpAttendances
         where (From == null || From == "" || EF.Functions.DateDiffDay(a.login, crdate) <= 0) &&
               (To == null || To == "" || EF.Functions.DateDiffDay(a.login, exdate) >= 0)
         group a by a.EmployeeName into g
         join c in db.Users on g.Key equals c.Id
         where (user == null || user == "" || c.Id == user)
         select new
         {
             id = g.Max(x => x.Id),   // representative attendance Id per employee; use g.Min if legacy FirstOrDefault returned the lowest
             user = c.UserName,
         });

This is fully server-translatable in EF Core 10: g.Key is the grouping key and g.Max(x => x.Id) is an aggregate, so no grouped-entity reference remains. It preserves the output shape { id (long), user (string) } and the one-row-per-EmployeeName cardinality. Apply the IDENTICAL rewrite to the two byte-identical siblings: EmployeeAttendanceController.cs:567-581 (which additionally has a .Distinct() before OrderBy â€” keep that) and ForecastController.cs:3857-3871. Recommend g.Max(x => x.Id) to match the typical "latest Id" representative; if exact parity with EF6's FirstOrDefault (which had no ORDER BY, so it returned an arbitrary but in practice often lowest row) is required, g.Min(x => x.Id) is equally translatable â€” either is output-preserving for the downstream search-by-id and the displayed user name.
reasoning:
I attempted to refute but the code positively confirms the break. File: QuickNetCore/Controllers/ShowRoomItemForecastController.cs, method GetReport(string user, string From, string To), lines 1213-1227.

SERVER-SIDE: db.EmpAttendances is a real DbSet<EmpAttendance> (Models/ApplicationDbContext.cs:515). v is built as a pure IQueryable (Where + dynamic OrderBy) and first enumerated at `recordsTotal = v.Count();` (line 1242) and `v.Skip(skip).Take(pageSize).ToList();` (line 1243). There is NO .ToList()/.AsEnumerable()/.AsAsyncEnumerable() boundary before enumeration, so the entire group/join/select runs in SQL. Confirmed server-side.

WHY IT 500s: The query does `group a by a.EmployeeName into g`, then `join c in db.Users on g.FirstOrDefault().EmployeeName equals c.Id` and projects `id = g.FirstOrDefault().Id`. g.FirstOrDefault() returns a whole grouped EMPTY/row entity â€” it is neither the grouping key nor an aggregate. EF Core 10's GroupBy translation only supports g.Key and aggregate functions (Count/Sum/Min/Max...) in the post-group projection; a non-aggregate group-element reference is untranslatable. Moreover it is used as a JOIN KEY, which must be translated to SQL and can never be client-evaluated. At v.Count() EF Core 10 throws InvalidOperationException ("could not be translated") -> HTTP 500. This is a TRANSLATE-TIME failure, so it fires on every request regardless of data â€” HIGH severity, not edge-only. (Note: the grouped IGrouping-is-never-empty SAFE rule does NOT apply here â€” the problem is not an empty group, it is that FirstOrDefault() over a server group is not an aggregate and cannot be translated.)

PORT REGRESSION: legacy EF6 at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/ShowRoomItemForecastController.cs:1290-1305 has the byte-identical query (only DbFunctions.DiffDays / JsonRequestBehavior.AllowGet differ from EF.Functions.DateDiffDay). EF6/LINQ-to-Entities translated grouped FirstOrDefault() to a correlated subquery; EF Core 10 does not. Genuine port regression.

CLAIM ACCURACY CAVEAT: the claim calls this the "third copy ... same as the other two GetReport copies" and says to apply the same fix as those. There is only ONE GetReport in this controller; the two siblings are EmployeeAttendanceController.cs:567 and ForecastController.cs:3857. All three are byte-identical and NONE has been fixed (all still contain raw g.FirstOrDefault()). The "fix already applied to the other two" framing is wrong, but this does not change the verdict for this location.

finalFix:
Mirror the established remediation in CreditSaleReturnNoteController.cs. Before the big `var v = (from a in db.PurchaseEntrys ...)` query (i.e. after line ~3048), materialize the approval rows once and build a client-side per-PurchaseEntry "latest status per ApprovedBy" lookup:

    // Materialize all PurchaseEntryPayment approval rows once, then compute latest-per-ApprovedBy client-side.
    var appUpdRows = db.ApprovalUpdates
        .Where(x => x.Type == "PurchaseEntryPayment")
        .Select(x => new { x.TransEntry, x.ApprovalStatus, x.ApprovedBy, x.CreatedDate })
        .ToList();
    var chkAppStatusLookup = appUpdRows
        .GroupBy(a => a.TransEntry)
        .ToDictionary(g => g.Key, g => g
            .GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy)
            .Select(grp => grp.OrderByDescending(c => c.CreatedDate).First())
            .Select(a => a.ApprovalStatus).ToList());

Then DELETE the server-side `let chkAppStatus = db.ApprovalUpdates.Where(...).GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(c => c.CreatedDate).FirstOrDefault()).ToList().Select(x => x.ApprovalStatus).ToList()` (lines 3064-3066) and the `chkAppStatus = chkAppStatus,` member at line 3119 from the server-side projection.

In the post-materialization `.ToList().Select(o => new {...})` block (line 3125+), inject the lookup. Since the `chkAppStatus` list is consumed at line 3152 in `ApprovalStatus = (... o.chkAppStatus.Count > 0 ...)`, restore it there. The simplest minimal edit: keep a server-projected key (the row already projects `a.PurchaseEntryId` as `o.PurchaseEntryId`), and in the outer Select compute:

    var chkAppStatus = chkAppStatusLookup.TryGetValue(o.PurchaseEntryId, out var ck) ? ck : new List<ApprovalStatus>();

then use `chkAppStatus` in place of `o.chkAppStatus` in the ApprovalStatus expression at line 3152.

This preserves identical output (latest ApprovalStatus per ApprovedBy for that PurchaseEntry, as List<ApprovalStatus>) and matches legacy EF6 semantics, while moving the untranslatable GroupBy-row projection to client memory. Apply the same fix to the two sibling occurrences in this file (the other GetPurchaseEntryPayment* grid actions that carry the identical `let chkAppStatus` at the legacy-mirrored positions). Do NOT use the proposed `.AsEnumerable()`-before-GroupBy approach â€” it does not create a valid client boundary inside a correlated server-side subquery.

Replace lines 4144-4177 with:

  var dt = System.DateTime.Now.AddYears(2);
  var amcremarsk = (from a in db.AddedRemarks
                    where a.TransactionType == "amcremarks"
                    select new { a.TransactionId, a.CreatedDate, a.nexttime })
                   .AsEnumerable()                              // materialize before grouped FirstOrDefault
                   .GroupBy(r => r.TransactionId)
                   .Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(r => r.CreatedDate).First())  // group never empty
                   .Select(r => new { r.TransactionId, r.CreatedDate, nexttime = r.nexttime ?? dt })
                   .ToList();

  var curdate = System.DateTime.Now;
  // window filter done in memory to mirror DateDiffHour(curdate, nexttime) < 12
  var amcMap = amcremarsk
      .Where(d => (d.nexttime - curdate).TotalHours < 12)
      .ToDictionary(d => d.TransactionId, d => d.nexttime);
  var amcIds = amcMap.Keys.ToList();

  var rem = (from a in db.Amcs
             join b in db.AmcAssignedTos on a.AmcId equals b.AmcId
             join c in db.Customers on a.CustomerId equals c.CustomerID
             where amcIds.Contains(a.AmcId)
                && b.Status == "Assigned" && b.ChkStatus == Status.active
             select new { b.EmployeeId, a.AmcId, taskname = a.AmcNo + "-" + c.CustomerName })
            .Distinct().ToList()

            .ToList();

If you need to match SQL DATEDIFF(hour,...) truncation exactly, use (int)System.Math.Floor((d.nexttime - curdate).TotalHours) or compute hour-boundary difference; the simple TotalHours form preserves the intended 'within 12h' behavior.

breakClass: left-join null member access / non-nullable value-column read on a possibly-null DefaultIfEmpty() entity in a server-side projection (EF Core 10 shaper reads SQL NULL into a non-nullable CLR value type â€” decimal/int/DateTime/enum â€” and throws; EF6 silently produced default(T))

    SellingPrice = h != null ? h.ItemUnitPrice : 0m,
    ItemQuantity = h != null ? h.ItemQuantity : 0m,
    sellingcost  = h != null ? h.ItemQuantity * h.ItemUnitPrice : 0m,
    ...
    purchasecost = h != null ? h.ItemQuantity * a.PurchasePrice : 0m,
    profit       = h != null ? (h.ItemQuantity * h.ItemUnitPrice) - (h.ItemQuantity * a.PurchasePrice) : 0m

Notes: (1) line 10134 currently uses the anonymous-member shorthand `h.ItemQuantity` (member name inferred as ItemQuantity) â€” it must be rewritten to the explicit `ItemQuantity = h != null ? h.ItemQuantity : 0m` so the property name is preserved for the JS/DataTables consumer. (2) The ternary `h != null ? ... : 0m` translates server-side in EF Core 10 to a CASE WHEN that yields 0 instead of reading NULL into the non-nullable decimal, reproducing EF6's default(decimal) exactly. (3) `e.ItemUnitName`/`f.ItemUnitName` (left joins on ItemUnits) are string (nullable-reference) and are safe â€” strings already shape NULL fine; no change needed there. (4) If product intent were to EXCLUDE unsold items, an alternative is adding `&& h != null` to the where-clause, but that changes the row set vs legacy; the guarded-projection above preserves the legacy output and is the minimal fix.

The `select new { ... item = h.ItemCode + "-" + h.ItemName, ItemQuantity = (h.SubUnitId==g.ItemUnit)?g.ItemQuantity:g.ItemQuantity/h.ConFactor, UnitPrice=h.PurchasePrice ...}` projection is composed into the IQueryable `v` that is enumerated by `.ToList()` at line 110. There is NO `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()` boundary before it, so the projection is translated to SQL and run server-side. It is NOT a standalone top-level query and NOT a group-aggregate over an IGrouping â€” the later `group...by` query `v2` (lines 111-140) runs over the already-materialized in-memory list `v`, so the "group never empty" exemption does not apply to this projection.

NULL h is reachable: `h` (Items) is joined via `join h in db.Items on g.Item equals h.ItemID into temp2 from h in temp2.DefaultIfEmpty()` â€” an explicit LEFT JOIN. Entity Item.PurchasePrice is non-nullable decimal (Models/Item.cs line 27) and Item.ConFactor is non-nullable decimal (line 85). g (MaterialRequisitionItems) is also `DefaultIfEmpty()`-joined, and g.Item is a non-nullable long FK with NO enforced referential integrity (ApplicationDbContext configures FKs only for RoleGroup; the model uses manual joins with shadow FKs stripped, lines 710-739). So h is NULL when (a) a MaterialRequisitionItem references a deleted/orphaned Item, or (b) a MaterialRequisition has no items at all (g NULL -> g.Item NULL -> h NULL). The WHERE clause does not exclude these rows (the h.ItemName.Contains(srchtxt) predicate is guarded by `srchtxt=="" ||`, so on the default empty search it never filters NULL h).

EF6 silently materialized default(decimal)=0. Caveat on the hunter's proposed fix: it only guards UnitPrice=h.PurchasePrice and MISSES the equally-fatal non-nullable read h.ConFactor on line 95 (g.ItemQuantity / h.ConFactor). A NULL-h row crashes on whichever column the shaper reads first; guarding only PurchasePrice would still 500. The complete output-preserving fix must null-guard both decimal reads (h.PurchasePrice and h.ConFactor), mirroring EF6's default(decimal)=0. h.SubUnitId is long? so the `h.SubUnitId == g.ItemUnit` comparison is itself null-tolerant, and h.ItemCode/h.ItemName are strings (null-safe in concatenation), so those need no change.

breakClass: left-join null member access / non-nullable value-column read on a possibly-null DefaultIfEmpty() entity in a server-side projection (EF Core 10 shaper reads SQL NULL into a non-nullable CLR value type â€” decimal/int/DateTime/enum â€” and throws; EF6 silently produced default(T))
why: c=Customers left join; Customer.CreditPeriod is non-nullable int. Walking/orphaned customer -> c NULL -> 500. Server-side. Legacy identical. Representative of the CreditPeriod=party.CreditPeriod class (CreditSale/ProForma/PurchaseOrder/Common.cs â€” see notes). MED.
finalFix:
In QuickNetCore/Controllers/DeliverynoteController.cs, line 1358, change:

    CreditPeriod = c.CreditPeriod,

to:

    CreditPeriod = c != null ? c.CreditPeriod : 0,

This mirrors the legacy EF6 behavior (which silently yielded default(int)=0 for the unmatched/NULL left-joined customer). The `c != null` guard is translated by EF Core into a CASE/null-check so the non-nullable int is never read from a SQL NULL, preserving the original output (0) for walking/orphaned-customer delivery notes while keeping the real CreditPeriod for matched customers. The same one-line guard should be applied to the sibling CreditPeriod = c.CreditPeriod sites in Models/Common.cs (lines 8625, 11308, 12365, 12589, 12807) which share the identical left-join-then-read pattern.
reasoning:
I independently confirmed the flag rather than refuted it. Location: QuickNetCore/Controllers/DeliverynoteController.cs, method Details(long? id), line 1358: `CreditPeriod = c.CreditPeriod`.

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” CONFIRMED. The projection is part of one continuous LINQ query (`from b in db.Deliverynotes ... select new DeliverynoteViewModel {...}`) whose only materialization boundary is `.FirstOrDefault()` at line 1382. There is no prior `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()`/`.AsAsyncEnumerable()` and it is not a standalone top-level local query nor a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. The whole select is translated to SQL, so `c.CreditPeriod` is read by the EF Core shaper from the SQL result.

(b) WOULD 500 ON REALISTIC/EDGE DATA â€” CONFIRMED. `c` originates from `join c in db.Customers on b.Customer equals c.CustomerID into cust / from c in cust.DefaultIfEmpty()` (lines 1328-1329), i.e. an optional (left-joined) entity that is NULL when no customer matches. `Customer.CreditPeriod` is a NON-nullable `int` (Models/Customer.cs line 26). `Deliverynote.Customer` is a non-nullable `long` FK, and the same projection's PayType branch (`b.CustomerType == CustomerType.Walking ? "Cash" : "Credit"`) plus the CustomerType enum (General.cs: Walking/Card = cash) prove walking/cash-sale delivery notes â€” which carry no real Customer row â€” are an expected, real scenario. For such a row the left join yields no match, `c` is NULL, and the SQL column for `c.CreditPeriod` comes back NULL. EF Core 10's shaper reads that SQL NULL into the non-nullable CLR `int` and throws (InvalidOperationException / null-at-ordinal cast failure) -> HTTP 500. EF6's legacy ObjectQuery shaper silently produced default(int)=0, so the page rendered.

PORT REGRESSION â€” CONFIRMED. The legacy EF6 source at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/DeliverynoteController.cs (line 1580, inside the identical Details query with the same `from c in cust.DefaultIfEmpty()` left join) is byte-identical: `CreditPeriod = c.CreditPeriod`. The code was not changed during the port; only the EF runtime behavior changed. This is a faithful member of the CreditPeriod=party.CreditPeriod class noted in project memory (batch 27 / CreditSale/ProForma/PurchaseOrder/Common.cs counterparts at Common.cs lines 8625, 11308, 12365, 12589, 12807, etc.).
